---
layout: post
title: "The Answer Came Back as an Error"
subtitle: "HTB Reactor, where a single quiet React page hides a deserializer that builds a function out of your words, the box answers only when it fails so you make failure carry the loot, and a debugger left running as root turns one command into a crown"
date: 2026-07-16 12:00:00 +0000
description: "HTB Reactor walkthrough. A Next.js single-page app whose only door is the React Flight protocol: CVE-2025-55182 deserialization RCE, error-based exfil, and a root Node inspector. Sealed while the box is live."
image: /assets/og/reactor.png
tags: [hackthebox, writeup]
---

Reactor shows you one static page and dares you to find a second one. There is no login, no API, no hidden vhost, and that emptiness is the whole message, because when a modern React app has no routes to knock on, the door stops being a page and becomes the protocol underneath it. The way in is a bug in React itself, a deserializer that will build a running function out of a string you control. The clever part is not the code execution, it is how you hear back from it. The box will not phone home, so you phrase your request as something the framework has to refuse, and it prints your answer inside its own error. From there it is a reused password sitting unsalted in a file and a debugger someone forgot to switch off.

```
        r e a c t o r            (medium · linux)
        =========================
        one page.  no routes.  no forms.
        so the door is the protocol, not a path.
                        |
                        v
        you hand it data.  it builds a function.
        you ask for output. the box stays quiet.
        so you make it fail, and read the failure.
                        |
                        v
        every lock past that is somebody trusting
        an unsalted hash, a reused password, or a
        debug flag to be the harmless thing it looked like.
                                              . . .
```

The full chain, from the one quiet page to root, is written up below in the usual detail. Because Reactor is **still active on Hack The Box**, that write-up is sealed. It unseals the moment the box retires. If you already hold the passphrase, the gate is right here.

<script type="application/json" id="lock-data">{"v":1,"iter":250000,"salt":"f9KRJSXydqNYM4aIuso2UQ==","iv":"BrwlseLealEau+xz","ct":""}</script>

{% include locked-writeup.html %}

---

*HTB: Reactor, a medium Linux box that is really a lecture on deserialization, exfil through the error channel, and the debug flags people leave burning in production; still active, and the full chain unseals here the day it retires, no live payloads either way.*
